title: Packing Codes for Other Data
text: Packing Codes for Other Data String, Image, and Raw data can be mapped to a variety of packing codes, including W for Unicode characters. Use the String data type for characters in any supported character sets. For more information, see Meta Character Sets . Use the Raw data type for fields containing raw data that should not be converted or processed. For example, raw is the data type to use for pictures and sound. Use the Image data type for fields that contain images from the database, glyphs, disk files, or third-party filters. If the underlying DBMS does not support the storage format determined by the packing code, the DBMS connector maps the information to the most suitable alternative. For more information, see the relevant connector documentation for your DBMS. Uniface Data Type Default Packing Code Image R Raw R String C40 Table 1. String, Raw, and Variable Packing Codes Uniface Data Types Uniface Packing Code Explanation String Raw Image C (C40), C1-C* Character string Y Y Y R (R40), R1-R* Binary (raw) Y Y Y S (S40), S1-S* Character string Y Y Y SC (SC40),SC1 SC* Segmented character string Y Y Y SR (SR40), SR1-SR* Segmented binary (raw) Y Y Y SU (SU40), SU1-SU* Segmented TRX character string Y Y Y U (U40), U1-U* TRX character string Y Y Y VC (VC40), VC1-VC* Variable-length character string Y Y Y VR (VR40), VR1-VR* Variable-length binary (raw) Y Y Y W Unicode characters Y When a packing code is used without a length or ‘*’, Uniface assumes the default length, which is shown in parentheses following the packing code. Limitations No arithmetic operations can be applied to this kind of data. You cannot use a retrieve profile for unlimited variable-length fields or for fields with the U packing codes. You cannot use an unlimited variable-length field or a Raw field as a key. Related Links Database Connectors To access, retrieve, and store data, Uniface provides database connectors that translate Uniface I/O requests into the target database's data definition and manipulation languages.
